policy_statement: By 2030, achieve at least 80% of trips by low-carbon modes; electrify at least 25% of registered private vehicles by 2030 and 100% by 2040.
mechanism_description: The policy sets goals to increase trips taken by low-carbon modes and to increase private vehicle electrification, which together reduce transportation greenhouse gas emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Transport demand + vehicle technology shift → ↓ fossil fuel combustion (and upstream fuel emissions) → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: Travel behavior and transport system supply shift trips from single-occupant, internal-combustion vehicles to low-carbon modes (walking, cycling, public transit, shared mobility) and increase the share of electric private vehicles for remaining car trips.  
2) Mediating physical process: Reduced gasoline/diesel combustion lowers direct CO₂ (and some CH₄/N₂O) emissions; electrification shifts energy use to the power sector where emissions are typically lower per km and can decline further as the grid decarbonizes.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in lifecycle transport-sector GHG emissions (primarily CO₂), lowering contribution to global warming.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced transportation GHG emissions by decreasing fossil-fuel vehicle kilometers traveled and replacing remaining internal-combustion travel with electricity.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The core pathway is reducing fossil-fuel use in transportation via (a) fewer/highly efficient car trips and (b) electrifying remaining private vehicle travel, yielding lower GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While there can be co-benefits (air quality, congestion, safety, energy efficiency), the dominant climate effect across policies using this mechanism is direct mitigation through emissions reductions, not primarily adaptation, resource efficiency as an end in itself, or nature-based carbon sequestration.  
3) Edge cases applied: If electricity is very carbon-intensive, EV benefits can be smaller, but the intended and typical dominant climate pathway remains transport GHG mitigation; mode shift still generally reduces emissions even under a high-carbon grid.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy combining sustainable mode shift and/or fleet electrification is fundamentally aimed at lowering transport combustion-related GHG emissions, so Mitigation remains the correct primary category for all such instances.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any further categorization (e.g., “public transit expansion” vs “EV incentives” vs “pricing/management”) depends on the specific policy design and evidence at the individual policy level and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If implemented mainly for congestion/air-quality with negligible net GHG change (e.g., EV uptake powered by high-emissions electricity and no VMT reduction), mitigation impact could be reduced, but the mechanism’s dominant climate-relevant intent and typical effect remains emissions reduction.
key_indicators: “By 2030”; “at least 80% of all trips”; “low-carbon modes”; “electrification of vehicles”; “at least 25% of all private vehicles registered”; “by 2040… 100%”
co_benefits: Air quality and public health benefits (reduced tailpipe pollution, more walking/cycling), congestion reduction, noise reduction, potential household fuel-cost savings.
instance_edge_case_notes: Although the goals imply potential future regulatory or investment actions, the provided excerpt itself contains targets only and does not specify enforcement mechanisms or programs; therefore classified as target-setting (enabling) rather than a mandate/standard.
